reasoning: The Lean statement asserts the non-summability of `1/i` (as ℚ) indexed by the subtype of squarefree integers in ℤ. While the subtype `{p : ℤ // Squarefree p}` includes negative squarefree integers and ±1 (not just positive squarefree naturals as in the informal proof's S ⊂ ℕ*), Mathlib's `Summable` requires unconditional convergence, which for real/ℚ-valued series coincides with absolute summability. Thus "not summable over all squarefree integers in ℤ" is equivalent to "not absolutely summable" which is equivalent to "∑ 1/n over positive squarefree integers diverges" — the informal claim. The ℚ codomain is also fine, since non-absolute-summability in ℝ forces non-summability in the rationals. This matches the ground-truth formalization (proofnet-281.lean) verbatim, which is proved by restricting along an injection from `Nat.Primes` and invoking `Nat.Primes.not_summable_one_div`.
